And before I show you how that all works, I want to just tell you a teeny little bit about me. Because I think it will help and especially help you relate to how I got here and how you could be on this stage, too. I founded my company in 2004, and back then it was literally just a big idea and a business card and an extra bedroom in my house. That was it. And that is part of the beauty of this moment in time. You can build a business with nothing more than that. I think of that as the democratization of live events that in our online world all you need is a computer and a big idea. And that's how I started. And honestly for me at the time, and I want to just kind of ask you those folks back at home, if this relates to you. For me it was what I would call a just until business. It was a just until business because I didn't identify as an entrepreneur at that time. I identified as a wife married to a man whose family business had imploded and when it imploded, he imploded. And I was the one who paid all the bills. So I was calculating all the money and I'm like, this is not sustainable. And so I'm like, you know what? I think I could create this business just until he gets back on his feet again. It was a just until business. How many of you can relate to it's a just until business? Raise your hand if you have a just until business or it started that way. It started as I'll just do this just until this other thing happens. Well, I want to tell you that it goes from just until to a just because business. I do this just because it matters that much. And I was pretty lucky because in my second year I was introduced to Glaser Kennedy Insider Circle. Bill Glaser and Dan Kennedy, they're a friend of a friend. One client at the time and a good friend of mine said, I think you need to meet Bill Glaser and Dan Kennedy. Why don't you come to the super conference with me? Now this was back in 2004. I did not know what information marketing was. I had no idea what a super conference was. And I will tell you if I'm being really honest, I got to that event with the catty Wampus. You know, I don't know how many of you have been to this, but back then it was literally a big sign, I mean, a hung sideways with some really bad lighting and some really bad staging. And this man comes out on stage and he is in a satine shirt. It's satin on satin with a satine suit, satin on satin stripe with a bolero and a handlebar mustache. And I am like, where am I? How desperate am I that this is going to be my second client? I mean, I'm just thinking, how do I let him down gently? I'm not this broke. There's got to be more for me. I deserve better stages. I create prettier events. And then he started talking. And I'll never forget this. He said, how much would you pay for this paperback book? How much would you pay for a paperback book? Drop it in the chat. How much would you pay? How much would you pay for a paperback? Put it in here so that I can see it. Yeah, $999, $14.99, $5, $7.00. Back in 2004, those were the same answers. And then he held up a hardback book and he said, how much would you pay for this book? How much would you pay for a hardback? 20, 15, maybe you get 25, maybe you get 60, maybe. And then he held up what I would call a coffee table book, a big, beautiful coffee table book. How much would you pay for this? How much would you pay for that? Drop it in the chat. Number seven, change much. This is something about being an author to note. Has not changed much. That's exactly right. And then he held up a tattered, spiral-bound, coffee-stained book. And he said, how much would you pay for this? And I can tell you what went in my head is a $1.99. And he said, $19.99. And I'm like, oh, wow, you get $20 for that? That's incredible. And then he goes, no, no, $1,999. And I'm like, now you have my attention. And I had always wanted to be an author. So when I did the math on what it would take for a paperback book, and you know how hard it is to get a successful paperback or hardback, and then here's this tattered, terrible-looking thing, $19.99. And again, the prices haven't changed that much. It's what's inside that. And you guys get that because you're here. It's what's inside that book. And I remember sitting there and thinking, I can do that. I know enough to create my own book. I could do that. In fact, I'm going to do that by the end of the day. I had so many notes. That event changed my life. I became their event planner. And then I became somebody else's and somebody else's because they're like, whoever's doing Glaser Kennedy's events, they look markedly better. I did change those crappy signs. I really did. They look markedly better. And they're making more money at the back of the room. And I never had to market. It was a referral-based business from that point on. And luck, I mean, just, you know, a mix of fortune, blessing, and hard work. But I had a referral-based business that, before I knew it, was seven figures. And it was all referral-based. And it was all in this space. It was all in the space of high ticket offers and, at that time, in-person live events. And so I want you to know that whether your offer is a thousand or 10,000 or 15,000 or 25,000 or 100,000 or 500,000, it doesn't matter what size offer it is. At the end of the day, you're going to have to know how to overcome objections, your own and the people around you. And that's what we're going to talk about for any size offer. What do you need to know? But first, I wanted to spell something really quickly. You might be thinking, okay, in the last 20 years, you've helped generate over $2 billion in sales for your clients from high ticket offers and live events, you were probably born with mad sales skills. It probably comes naturally to you. You're probably really, really good at sales, and that's how you grew this. Really from nothing to something extraordinary, to time and money freedom, to a lot of purpose-driven paydays. And here's the thing, to build my eight-figure business, I had to master sales. And I was not good at it. In fact, to tell you just how bad I was at it, I actually quit the Girl Scouts, because I did not want to sell cookies. Now, you have to understand that I loved the Girl Scouts. At that age in my life, in fact, I think we have a picture. The guys are going to put it up, I think, of me at the age where, oh yeah, here's little Barry. And here's what I love about little Barry. Even then, she knew, you don't go to school without a good person, you don't go anywhere without a good person, good shoes. So this is me at that age. And I love Girl Scouts. I love the little outfits, I love the little cookies, I love the activities, but when they said, you have to sell cookies, I'm like, who knew? I'm out. Now, here's what I want you to think about, and folks at home, I want you to lean in for this. If my Girl Scout leader had come to me and said, Barry, before you quit on yourself, let's talk about this. Really, lean in here. What's your favorite Girl Scout cookie? And I would say, Thin Mints, because the Thin Mints. And then she said, listen, all you have to do is say, hey, would you like to try a Thin Mints? And be like, well, I can do that. Everybody should try a Thin Mints. It's the best cookie ever. And then she'd say, what do you think they'd say after they tried it? And I would say, can I have another? And then she would say, Barry, that's how this works. When they say, can I have another, you say one box or two? One box or two. If she had known that, she would not have quit Girl Scouts. She would have kept all of her friends, done all the things she loved doing, and she probably would have been the top selling Girl Scout, like no doubt about it. That one box or two mindset is at the heart of the sales and service approach I'm going to share with you. And it's important because you don't have to be good at sales. You can actually be petrified of sales. You can want to do anything but sell, and what I'm going to share with you is going to work with you because it's coming from a place of service and believing in yourself and what you're meant to do. You are a prime mover. You already claimed it. And in order to be a prime mover and change the world, you've got to get out there with the thing you're meant to do and the people you're meant to serve. And this works with all of our clients. It doesn't matter if it's Tony Robbins or Russell Brunson or Dean Graziosi or Jeff Walker, Amy Porterfield or Matthew McConaughey, all of whom we've designed offers for, all with the sales and service approach. Enrollment theory is at the heart of every event, every offer, and every conversation. And the good news is, like selling one to many, it's a learnable skill. It's a learnable skill that you can learn today and apply to any situation. Now, I want to be honest with you. Like any really good tool, repetition matters. It is actually a muscle that you have to build. You have to exercise it every single day. It allowed me to build my serve hard, sell easy framework by finding a natural conversation that I can sell one to many without feeling icky, feeling salesy, or feeling sleazy. Now, to do this, there are just three simple things that you need to learn for the sales and service framework. The first is understanding there are two types of buyers. There are two types. I'm going to explain what they are in just a minute. The second is knowing the right questions to ask any prospect. What are the questions that you need to ask in order to start the conversation? It's easier than you think. The third is having the ability to recognize, simplify, and slay any objection that comes into your way. Recognize it, simplify it, and slay it. Those are the three things we're going to talk about. So let's talk about the first one. There are two types of buyers, two. And as I'm saying this, I want you to think about which one is you. The first is an emotional buyer. An emotional buyer is someone that as soon as they hear an offer, their credit card is out. They're like, I got it, I'm in. Now, it could be because it's the perfect offer to solve the pain they're in. It could be that they're just at the right time in their life, or it could be that's just how they buy. I happen to be married to an emotional buyer. Blue Melnick has never met an offer he doesn't like. He has his credit card out before you've even finished the offer. That is who he is. That is how he's wired. We love emotional buyers. I think about 30% of the world is an emotional buyer. I think 70% is the other type of buyer, logical. And a logical buyer is one who asks all the questions. I'm a logical buyer. A logical buyer is someone who like recently I bought a new car. And when they gave me the paperwork, they expected me just to sign it. And I'm like, oh, no, no, not so fast. I want to read every line together and have you answer all my questions. Now, they're not going to change the contract for me. I'm still going to ask all the questions. Are you emotional or are you logical? Drop it in the chat. I'm emotional or I'm logical. Your buyers are going to fall into those two camps. And here's why this matters. Your objections come from logical buyers. Emotional buyers go straight to the back of the room. Emotional buyers click right on the sales page. They go right to the QR code. They're filling out the order form before you're even done. You're not going to talk to them. You're going to talk to logical buyers. Those are the ones that are going to come to you because they have questions. And so you might think, oh, no, this offer was terrible. All I'm getting are questions and objections. Instead of recognizing, oh, that's how this works. Logical buyers need to ask questions. So you have to flip the script. Don't make it personal. Don't see them as objecting or rejecting you or your offer. See them as navigating their own natural instinct to question everything. It's their way of processing and deciding. And if you are a logical buyer, you can recognize right now if you did that this weekend. Were you the person who immediately thought, sounds good. I have some questions. It doesn't mean you're not committed. Doesn't mean you can't get to yes or no. You're just a logical buyer. And once you recognize that, you can have empathy for the logical buyers in your room. Okay. The second thing is know the right questions to ask. Every sales conversation, every sales conversation, every sales conversation starts with three simple words. Just three words. You're going to write it down. You can use these at home. Are you ready? Tell me more. Tell me more. I'm going to answer. I can't do this because I want this. All these questions. Right. And your natural instinct is to rush to answer. Now, in any sales conversation, if you're doing most of the talking, you're already losing. Your job is to get them to talk to you. And the way you do that is an open-ended question. Tell me more. And this is what's really critical about this. Don't you want to know? Tell me more. Tell me more. Tell me more. Tell me more. Tell me more. Tell me more. Tell me what's so critical about this? Don't you want to know? Like, I think, if your prospect is your right fit client, the person you are most meant to work with, so if it's your right fit client, do you not want to know? Why are they asking these questions? So when they say I wish I could, I can't afford it, like, interesting? Tell me more about that. This is coming from a place of service. this, you want to know. And you can establish where they are on the continuum, because trust me, every one of these objections is on a continuum. And I'm going to explain exactly how that works, as I explain what the five objections are. But once you do tell me more, you can identify where they are on the continuum, and then you can have a service-based conversation around connection. Let me connect with you first. Diagnosis, let me diagnose what pain you're in. And then prescription. Let me prescribe the solution. And make no mistake, the solution is the problem you solve, a.k.a. your offer, which I like to think of as your invitation. I'd like to invite you to work with me further. So this leads me to the third thing, so that you can fully understand the continuum. How do you have the ability to recognize, simplify, and slay any objection that comes your way? It comes down to five simple objections. And that's good news for you, because once you know them, you can slay them. You can recognize them. You can simplify them, and you can slay them. How did I come up with the top five objections? 20 years of back-of-room sales. Now, I say this not to brag. I don't think anyone in this room, I don't think many people on the planet have been part of more offers or more sales conversations than I have. The reality is, for 20 years, we did one event, if not two events a week. And we were at the back of the room. We were training the sales teams. We were identifying the objections and figuring out how to overcome them. So what I'm going to tell you comes from making an exhaustive list of every objection we would get. I had a spreadsheet, and I was cataloging every objection, every that did matter if it was a real estate event, a restaurant event, a personal development event, did matter what kind of event it was, what are the objections. And then the list got so long that I'm like, ugh, this is exhausting. Ugh, I wonder if I put them into buckets. Hmm, these all feel like this. These all feel like this. The, oh, this fits over here. Oh, here's another bucket. Let's put those in here. And what I found out over the last 20 years is there are five buckets. And I've tried this in every niche, every language, every market you can possibly imagine. So if you're in the international crowd, this applies to you. We have tested it. Humans are humans, and buyers are buyers. This is true. Humans are humans, buyers are buyers. It doesn't matter what niche you're in. Don't say it doesn't apply to me. You are not a unique snowflake. There are five objections time tested over 20 years. Are you ready for what they are? Okay, again, lean in, come to me, and I want you to write these down. Time, money, spouse, fear, shame and self doubt. In that order, time, money, spouse, fear, shame and self doubt, those are the five. Now you know. So anytime you hear an objection, do you say, oh, no worries, that fits in the time bucket. That fits in the money bucket. And this is where the continuum comes in. For the continuum to work, I want you to say, tell me more. And then they say, oh, I wish I could. It's just, I'm working three jobs right now and taking care of my mother who has dementia and I have two young kids. I don't know how to find the time. And then somebody else says, oh yeah, I wish I could. But I think if I were gonna do this, I think I'd have to give up tennis on Saturday. Or, man, I think I'd have to do that after I put the kids to bed. Or maybe I'd have to barter with my husband. Maybe if he did this, I could do that on this day. Or maybe I'd have to take time off of work. Those are all time objections. They're radically different ends of the continuum. This is gonna be uncomfortable. This is virtually impossible or not the same objection. But they're both about time. Are you with me? Are you with me? Okay, great. Are you with me? So on money, let's address that one. I wish I could. I don't have the money. Where are they on the continuum? Oh, I wish I could. It's just, that's gonna be a really uncomfortable conversation. We were talking about not spending any more money until I made money. I don't know if I can do that. Oh, I need to do this. I really need to do this. But I've gotta tell you, I can barely put food on the table. We're barely making our rent. Those are both money objections. They're not the same objection, are they? You have to know where they are on the continuum. And the only way to find that out is to say, tell me more and mean it and listen to what they're telling you. And then apply what I'm gonna share with you right now. Okay. So let's go through them one by one. Time. Real talk. You can find time for things you really want. And you do. How much time do you spend on Netflix? How much time do you spend on social media on your phone? If you take out your phone, or if you have your right fit client, take out their phone and look, how much time are they wasting every day? And you negotiate with them. Hey, here's the thing. I bet it could help you find the time. Let's analyze where you're spending your time and let me see if I can help you find the time. And by the way, the things I'm gonna ask you to give up are not forever. You don't have to give up Netflix forever. Give it up for a season. Give it up for a few months while you really focus right here on what really matters, on what really moves the needle. Because if you focus that time right here right now, you will have a lifetime of being able to watch Netflix whenever you want. What is the real pain point? And what is the solution to solve it? Let's talk about money. My favorite one with money, and honestly it applies to time too, is you are more resourceful than your resources. Write that down. You are more resourceful than your resources. Meaning, no one is sitting around with a bucket of money, a bucket of time that is really rare. I just have so much time. I don't know where to spend it. I have so much money. I don't know what to do with it. The average person doesn't have time and doesn't have the money, but if you really want something, you are more resourceful than your resources. Let me give you an example. I tell them, take out a sheet of paper and write down one through 50, five, zero. One through 50 on the left-hand side, and brainstorm, what are all the ways that you could find the money? The magic is somewhere after 35. The first five are gonna be I could borrow it, I could get a zero interest credit card. In fact, these days you can ask AI, how can I make the money? Do that sometime, you will be shocked. Go, even Google, how do I make money? What are 50 ways that I could make fast cash? You will be shocked at how unbelievably efficient it is. Do you own a car? Do you own a car? Back at home, do you have a car? Raise your hand if you have a car. You could drive Uber. You could drive DoorDash. It's not your new job. It's a bank loan, and you have to look at it that way. I have a car, I can make money. Not forever, I'm not an Uber driver, not that there's anything wrong with that. I'm using this to fund the big dreams that I have. Are you willing to do that? Because if you're not, don't ask them to do it. Do you get that? Yeah, it's important. 50 ways, you're more resourceful than your resources. Let's talk about spouse. Now, with spouse, you can also use business partner, significant other. This is the person who holds sway over your decisions that lives in your home. Not your best friend or your college buddy, although that matters too. This is the person in your home. And I used to say that this was a pretty female thing, that we do what I'm about to tell you about. But more and more I find that men do it too, because we're in dual income households. Is we ask for permission, not support? And so, can I tell you what this sounds like? I just wanna say ahead of time, this is what I call level five conversation. A level five conversation is where I get really real with you. It's like think of it like a Garrett White conversation, just a little softer. So a level five conversation, I have permission, let me see, hands up? Yes, I can do this, we can go level five? All right, all right, not everybody's hand is up. Abid's not sure, Jane just reluctantly said yes. Go team go, Joan is all in, Ernest, thank you. All right, Virginia, thank you, okay. If I'm going to my spouse, and I approach it this way, hey, I wanna ask you about something. I was thinking about spending this money on this thing. There's this guy, this Russell Brunson guy. He's got this amazing thing he's figured out. It's really awesome. It's $10,000. Do you think I should do that? I don't know. I mean, it does seem like a lot of money. I'm debating, should I do it? Should I, I don't know. I wanna get your opinion, should I do this? Yeah, no, huh? Yeah, I remember that time, okay. Yes, I remember the time I bought the thing and didn't do the thing. Yes, I did that twice, yeah, I know. Yes, I do complain about my work every single day, yes. I know yesterday I said I hated it, but I didn't really mean it. I know, yes, it would make more sense to just go get a job. I get it. No, you're right, you're right, you're right. This is crazy. This is crazy. Who relates to that? Who is asking for permission? If you're being really honest, because if you are your right fit client is to, trust me on that, they are a mirror for you. Whatever you are doing, they are doing too. And the only reason I feel like I can have this level five with you is that I have been you. I have had that conversation and I've had those answers. Now let's try it a different way. Hey, real quick, I gotta talk to you about something really amazing. I just, I think you should record this. I need you to turn the TV off, you can watch it later. I hope you can tell by my energy, like I have had a total transformation this weekend. This is crazy, but it just dawned on me that I have been trying to do this thing that is significantly hard all by myself and I do not have the tools. And I keep beating myself up because I can't do it. And it's like, well, of course I can't do it. I don't know how to run a business. I wasn't born with that skill. And I was thinking about it, like, you know, we had to hire that lawyer. We didn't just hope he knew how to negotiate. We wanted someone who went to law school and was really good. He went to law school to do that. And remember when you had that heart thing and we looked for the best cardiovascular surgeon? We didn't just want someone who was born with those skills. We wanted someone who'd been trained and done the work. And it's kind of crazy, because I'm like beating myself up because I don't have these skills, but it's just like that. You have to learn them. And it was like, oh my God, that's amazing, even more amazing. There's a framework. I don't have to keep beating my head against the wall and trying to figure it out. There's a literal framework for this. And I know it is a lot to say that I want to invest to myself given some of the crazy. And here I have had like a little come to Jesus with myself and I just want to apologize. Because I know that I have been turning every one of our dinner conversations into a coaching conversation. And that's kind of crazy because you're not a coach. In fact, you're not even a business owner. And I don't say that with any disrespect. I just realized it's so grossly unfair to you. And the good news is there are people out here who do this and they're qualified to do it. And I can invest in myself and to get their support. And guess what? Dinner conversations can go back to being about us and the kids. So here's the thing. I hope you can see in me that this is different this time. I have new clarity. I have new certainty about what I need to do and how I need to do it. And I need to be surrounded by other people doing it too. So I hope you'll understand I have to do this. And I would love your support, but I have to do this. I need to do this. I cannot do this alone. And here's the thing. If you will support this, I would really appreciate it. And this is what I'll do for you in exchange. If you let me do this on Saturdays, then I will do this on Sundays. I will study on Saturday. You'll golf on Sunday. We're going to barter with the kids. After the kids go to bed, I'm going to do this work. And here's the thing. When I get my first sale, we're going to go celebrate at that dinner, that restaurant that you've been wanting to try. And when I hit my first offer, I'm taking us on a vacation because you've helped me get there. And I want to reward you for supporting me, for being all in on my big vision. Yes. Woo! That's how it works. These are enrollment conversations. There is a saying, the person with the most certainty wins the debate. You cannot go to someone with a lack of certainty and ask them to be more certain about your vision than you are. If you think about it, that is cuckoo cuckoo. That doesn't make sense. And yet we do it all the time. We do it with our mother. We do it with our sister. We do it with our best friend. We do it with a massive lack of certainty and expect them to be more certain than we are about our vision. And it is actually grossly unfair to them. The number of spouses who are vilified, he won't let me. She won't let me. Really? Or you won't let you. That's the level five part. Are you copping out and blaming your spouse for it? If you are, it's time to stop that. That will not be a good marriage, I promise you. You will start to resent them. And it will be a surprise if they don't start to resent you. It's not fair. You have a vision. You have to enroll yourself in the vision and then enroll the people around you. And here's the thing. If you have the kind of spouse who truly is not supportive, then you more than anybody need to be in a program with people who are. And that's true of your right fit client, too. You have to be willing to take a stand for that. They are a mirror for you. So let's talk about fear and shame and self-doubt, because this is the next big one. And they're kind of intertwined. And remember I said they work like this, time, money, spouse, fear, shame and self-doubt. The farther down we go, the harder it is to talk about. Truth. The decisions that are most being controlled are not time. That's just the easiest one to talk about. It's not money. We're amazingly comfortable with talking about how we don't have money these days. It can be spouse for sure, but more often you. But the fear, shame and self-doubt are actually most often controlling the mind chatter that is happening that's keeping you from making a decision. As Garrett said yesterday, are you in or are you out? Is it a yes or is it a no? The fear piece feels like I have failed before. I have failed before and I'm afraid of failing again. In fact, I'm also afraid of succeeding, because if I make a massive amount of money, am I still going to fit in with my friends? Is my spouse still going to love me? We are as afraid of failing as we are of succeeding. And that fear just holds sway over us. Like, what if it doesn't work? And Pedro Adeo, he's a good friend of mine. We talked about as a day of disgust. Until finally you get to a day of disgust, we're being controlled by fear is worse than doing the thing you're so afraid of. Don't wait for that day. Wrestle it to the ground right now. And here's the thing. For some of you, the fear piece kind of feels like this. Right now, the only thing keeping me going is the dream that this could happen. If I pursue it and I fail, then I've snatched the dream away. And it's the only thing keeping me going. I can't afford that. I can't afford to have that dream ripped away. It is the only thing keeping me going. What's going to happen if I'm not, I don't have what it takes. I'm not good enough. I'm not smart enough. I'm afraid of all those things. And you have to draw a line in the sand and say, I'm tired of fear controlling the conversation. Because right behind it is shame and self-doubt. And shame and self-doubt are the spiral that happens when you swing for the fences and you didn't get the home run. And it's kind of crazy. I want to break it down for you. If you think of our sports stars, like my husband I've been obsessed with the US Open this week and watching people is something else, right? And you think these are the best in class tennis players. And she missed that serve. Who led her on this court? I mean, isn't this her thing? Isn't she the best? How did she miss the serve? How did that ball go right by her? She is terrible. I cannot even believe she's out here. An Olympian who misses the perfect jump, perfect landing. How did they let them out here? A sports star that doesn't get the field goal every time. Do you think, what's he doing? We know in those cases that you don't win every time. Your past failures are not a predictor of your future success. And you have to be willing to say that to your right fit client. Tell me, what's really going on for you? Tell me more. Oh, I know that one. I've been there. Let me tell you what's on the other side of that. And a great analogy to use as a kid learning to walk. They stand up. They fall. They stand up. They fall. And you're the parent watching, and you're like, this is pathetic. Then you watch them kind of think about it. Oh, maybe if I crawl over to the sofa, and they fall again. And you're starting to think, maybe you're just not cut out for this, this walking thing? Not for you. I'm just, your dad and I are going to have to adjust our expectations. You're clearly going to crawl through life. It's OK. Crawling can work. Like, that's so asinine. We would never say that to a child. And yet, you say far worse things to yourself. Your right fit client says far worse things to themselves when they don't succeed every time. And here's the spoiler alert. When you swing for the fences, you don't always get the home run. In fact, there will be more times you don't than you do. It's how you respond to it. US Open, this girl American losing. She is what is happening in this first set. It was crushing how bad she was playing. Her mindset rallied, and she won. She had to believe in herself. She had to believe in the possibility, and you do too. Your past failure is not a predictor of your future success. So now, ask yourself, which objection is most likely to keep you stuck and make you quit? Which one is it? Drop it in the chat. If you're willing to be really honest, which one is most likely to make you quit? Internal divining rod, fear, money, all four, spouse, fear of failure, shame and self-doubt, money, fear. Zafin Chang, I got you. Yep. Right fit clients, a mirror for you. And so it has to start by you recognizing, simplifying, and slaying your own dragons before you can ask them to do that. And here's the thing. This is a writer downer. I want every one of you to write this down, not in the chat, someplace that you can see it, anytime you're in one of these conversations. Make your reason not to, your reason to. Now, let me tell you how this works. And to give credit where credit's due, I learned this from Lisa Sasevich, who's been a client of mine for years. I always like to give credit when I learn something from someone else. She's the one that said, make your reason not to, your reason to. If you give me any objection, I will tell you that's exactly why you have to do it. I don't have time. You're not going to magically make time. That's why you must do it. I don't have money. I can't afford to do it. That's why you need to do it. I don't have a spouse that supports me. Then please run. Don't walk. You must do it. I'm trapped in fear, shame, self-doubt. Then you of all people, please do not let a day become a decade. You have to do it. There's no objection you can give me, because my confidence is so strong. I know what's possible for my right fit clients. I can hold that space for them. I have been them. I have championed them. I've created success stories in them, just like Russell has. He can see things for you you can't see, because he's been there, and he's coached others there. And it's true for you too. When you're talking to your right fit clients, start with your own certainty. And you're thinking, oh, I'm not there yet. You won't get there if you don't start. Change begins with thinking, wait a minute. I can do this. Like that little Dan Kennedy seminar, where I'm like, 1999, $2,000, I could do that. And here's the other thing. A decision always comes down to a fork in the road. This is the last piece that you have to know about overcoming objections. Are you going to take the safe path or the risky path? Remember that first husband and that just until business I told you about? When I started that just until business out of a bedroom in my house, my husband really was imploding. He was drinking more at dinner than it was maybe we should have cocktails before dinner, then maybe we should have cocktails at lunch. This turned into a full on drinking problem. And I don't know if any of you have had to live through watching someone you really love, self-destruct, into a spiral of turning to alcohol, of them caring more about the bottle than they do about you. It is a painful thing. And I had started my business and I was going on a trip. And I remember saying to him, you know I love you, right? I'm going away for a week for this event. When I come back, you have to have decided. Are you going to quit this alcohol, or are you going to quit me? Because I can't continue doing this. I cannot go down with this ship. And I can't watch you do it either. So you have a week to think about it. Now, I'm not saying you have to have your life figured out, but you have to decide. And I went on the trip and I came home. And I remember walking in. I remember walking into the house and opening the front door and putting my bags down. And I looked at him. And if you've been in this situation, when you know, you know. I knew he'd already had a drink. And so I knew my answer too. And I remember we both just sat down and started crying because he knew I wasn't going to stand for that. That was not going to continue to work that way. And it was because I had started this just until business that I was able to take this next chapter of my life. And I said, I have to go. I'm sorry. I cannot stay for this. And he said, I know. And I went to my mom. And I'm like, you're not going to believe this, but I'm getting a divorce. I don't really believe in that. But I have to do this. And I'm going to move to New York City. Now, you have to know that I grew up in a tiny little house in a tiny little town, Virginia Beach. My neighborhood was called Pocahontas Village. You can Google it. Last time I did, it was like a crack sidewalk and a Budweiser. It was on literally the wrong side of the tracks over the railroad tracks. And there was my house. So I didn't grow up with the Silver Spoon. I didn't grow up thinking all these things are possible. But I did have a mother who really believed in Norma Vincent Peel in the power of positive thinking. And so she had imbued that in me. And I was like, listen, I want to move to New York City. I've always wanted to be there. And she's like, Barry, my mother, who had always chained me, always believed in me, was like, you can't move to New York City. I'm like, really? I mean, I've been watching Friends and Sex in the City. It's my job. They're my people. Like, I am meant to be there. I know that's not real, but it was getting me through. And she said, well, here's the thing. Your dad and I have this basement in the townhouse. And I think we could turn it into an apartment for you. While you're getting your life together, you can come and go. You'll have your own entrance. It's like we're not even there. And then you move to New York City. Now, I know for a fact that my mother did that out of an abundance of love for me. Our people who love us want to keep us safe. So so often when they're saying no and they don't have the same vision we do, it's not because they don't believe in us. It's because our vision has outpaced where they are. And they want to keep us safe. But I had this knowing, if I go to that basement, I'm not coming out. I'm going to die in there, literally or figuratively, I'm not sure. But a 38-year-old divorcee living in her parents' basement, no. And I did the hardest thing I've ever done. Mom, I know this is crazy. I've got to go to New York City. And I'll spare you all the details of how I got this tiny little amazing apartment on Broadway of all places. But it did involve enrollment theory. I enrolled my realtor. I enrolled that landlord hard. And I was lucky enough to get this amazing apartment. I remember going to, from DC, where my family was, to New York on the train with an air bed in a bag. And it was like a rolled-up air bed and a sheet and some toothpaste, toothbrush, a change of clothes. And my furniture was going to show up the next day. So I'm getting on the train with my air bed. I'm starting my new life in New York City. And I remember literally to this day, putting the key in the lock and turning it and swinging that door open. And the smell of those wood floors and that old, amazing New York apartment and looking out those corner windows up Broadway of all things and hearing all the sound of like taxis and noise on the street. I didn't know a soul in New York City. It was the most expensive city in the country. My business had just gotten started. I was still paying the bills for my husband and what he was doing. I did not look good on paper to my landlord. I didn't look good on paper to anybody. But I just knew I meant for more. I just knew I am a prime mover. I am meant to move the world. I am meant to change the world. I don't fully know how it looks yet or how it works, but I know I meant to do this. And with that certainty, I spread out that air bed and put those sheets on and climbed into it and had the most amazing sleep of my life. Because I was living on my terms, my way, no more compromise, no more sacrifice. It's up to you, girl. You do this. You have the power to do this. You decide how you show up. You decide what you do. You decide. And I did. I worked my butt off that year. The only day I took off was Saturday. And I worked 20 hours a day every day, no exaggeration. But it is also because of that that I met my husband, Blue, who is the most amazing man and business partner and life partner. And I can assure you he would not have fallen in love with a girl who lived in a basement. He's a good man. He's not that good a man. We have to be who we're meant to be to attract who we're meant to attract. And that's true for our spouses as much as it is for our colleagues as much as it is for right fit clients. Today, we have the most amazing life. We've built an incredible eight figure business together. And I want to show you, I think the team is going to pull up this picture. This is the house in Pocana's village that I grew up in. Somebody added the fence in the RV. We did not have that. The railroad tracks are right there. And when Blue and I got together and started to build our business together, and we decided finally we needed a brick and mortar office, we decided to move from Manhattan. And we moved to this house. We'd always wanted to live on the water. And we were like, if we're not going to live in the best city in the world, Manhattan, then we're going to live in the best water in the world. We're going to wake up every day and look at the sunrise and the sunset and go out on our boat. And then when COVID hit and we decided to switch from in-person to virtual events and have our own studio, we're like, it's hard to attract people to Merle's Inlet. We need to move to Charleston. And so we bought this house, which I think is a beautiful mix of city and province, two of our other favorite places. And so now we have the beach house and the city house. And I share this with you not because I need to brag about where I am. I share this with you because if I can start there and end up here, you can too. What kind of world does it happen in? This one. And what does it take to do it? Believing that you're a prime mover and taking the action to do it, to take your just until business and make it a just because business. You start to realize that the risky version is the safe one. Is it safe to live in the basement? Or is that the riskiest thing you could do? Is it risky to go to Broadway? Or is it the safest thing you could do? To bet on yourself and your big dreams and your internal knowing of where you're supposed to be and what you're supposed to do. Getting stuck in the basement is risky. And the real risk is regret. You can write that down. A lot of you are in a fork in the road moment right now. Is it the basement, whatever your version of that is, or is it Broadway, whatever your version of that is? You're one of those choose moments like Garrett talked about yesterday. Yes or no?
